title: Two Sum II Input Array Sorted
prompt: Given a sorted array `numbers` and an integer `target`, return the 1-indexed positions `[i, j]` of the two values whose sum is `target`. In Python, access `numbers` normally with 0-based indexing; only the returned positions are 1-indexed.
constraints: Input array is sorted in non-decreasing order
Exactly one solution exists; return 1-indexed positions, not 0-indexed Python offsets
scaffold:
def two_sum_ii_input_array_sorted(numbers: list[int], target: int) -> list[int]:
    pass
expectedApproach: Use two pointers: start one index at the left end and one at the right end. Because the array is sorted, move the left index rightward when the sum is too small and the right index leftward when the sum is too large. Return 1-indexed positions after finding the pair.
commonPitfalls: Mixing up 0-based Python indexing with 1-based returned positions
Using a nested loop instead of the sorted-array two-pointer approach
Forgetting to justify why sorting lets you move one side at a time
idealSolutionOutline: Explain the two-pointer invariant, implement one while-loop scan, return `[left + 1, right + 1]`, and summarize O(n) time / O(1) extra space.
evaluationNotes: Reward candidates who keep list access 0-based in Python, return 1-indexed positions, and clearly explain why the sorted order makes two pointers optimal.
